data.title: Pinnacle Financial, Synovus Financial Agree to $8.6B Merger
data.description:
By Freddy Sebastian

Pinnacle Financial Partners and Synovus Financial both agreed to merge in an all-stock deal valued at $8.6 billion.

The deal represents an approximate 10% premium to Synovus share price on an unaffected basis. The transaction is also expected to add about 21% to Pinnacle's estimated operating EPS in 2027.

The companies said on Thursday the deal will create the highest-performing regional bank focused on the fastest-growth markets in the Southeast.

Write to Freddy Sebastian at freddy.sebastian@wsj.com

data.title: FED-TRUMP SIDELIGHT: Presidential tour of Fed HQ reconstruction site keeping central bank independence in play but lowers the dispute temperature
data.description: Fed Chair Powell, forced to appear with President Trump framed by the reconstruction project, was obviously dismayed at the sheet of paper Trump handed him outlining the $3.1 billion cost, saying, no, the cost didn't just cover the main building, but also the past rehabilitation of the Eccles building next door and one other building. As the news cameras recorded the scene, with Trump answering a shouted question by saying he wanted Powell to lower interest rates, "What can I say?" history was recording another footnote in the decades-long saga of institutional tension between the executive branch and the institution designed to withstand political pressure. There seemed to be no middle ground between Trump's stated desire for a 1% fed funds rate immediately and the intention of the 19 participants in the policy -setting Federal Open Market Committee to make small cuts later in the year from a funds rate of 4.25% to 4.5%. "We would save a trillion dollars a year," Trump repeated after the tour if the interest rates were 3 percentage points lower. He said, "There was no tension" with the Fed chief he has criticized relentlessly. After the tour Trump conceded the complexities of the headquarters project, being built over a hidden water source, made the rebuilding more expensive. Trump, familiar with construction topics, seemed to lower the temperature, saying of Powell, "I think he's going to do the right thing." He repeated, he has no intention of firing Powell. Nevertheless, the episode kept the question alive, will the next Fed chair he installs next year be someone he expects to follow orders from the Oval Office?

data.title: VERISIGN INC/CA SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Verisign Inc., a global leader in domain name registry services and internet infrastructure, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance and significant operational achievements, underscoring its continued growth and market leadership.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenues: $409.9 million, increased by 6% compared to the same period in 2024, primarily due to .com and .net price increases.
  • Operating Income: $280.7 million, representing an increase of 5% compared to the same period in 2024.
  • Net Income: $207.4 million, reflecting a 4% increase from $198.8 million in the same period in 2024.
  • Basic EPS: $2.21, compared to $2.01 in the same period in 2024, indicating improved profitability per share.
  • Diluted EPS: $2.21, compared to $2.01 in the same period in 2024, consistent with the basic EPS improvement.

Business Highlights

  • Geographical Performance: The company reported revenue growth across all regions during the three and six months ended June 30, 2025, with the majority of growth generated from registrars based in the U.S. and EMEA. U.S. revenues increased by 5%, EMEA by 12%, APAC by 4%, and other regions by 5% compared to the same periods in 2024.
  • Sales Units: As of June 30, 2025, the company had 170.5 million .com and .net registrations in the domain name base, representing a slight decrease from June 30, 2024, but a net increase of 0.7 million domain name registrations from March 31, 2025. During the three months ended June 30, 2025, 10.4 million new domain name registrations for .com and .net were processed, compared to 9.2 million in the same period in 2024.
  • Renewal Rates: The final .com and .net renewal rate for the first quarter of 2025 was 75.5%, an improvement from 74.1% in the first quarter of 2024.
  • Future Outlook: The company intends to continue paying a cash dividend on a quarterly basis, subject to market conditions and approval by the Board of Directors. Additionally, the company is evaluating the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, enacted on July 4, 2025, on its financial statements.

SEC Filing: VERISIGN INC/CA [ VRSN ] - 10-Q - Jul. 24, 2025

data.title: OCEANEERING INTERNATIONAL INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Oceaneering International Inc., a global provider of engineered services and products primarily to the offshore energy industry, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant improvements in both financial and operational performance, driven by strong contributions from various business segments and recent contract awards.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $698.2 million, reflecting an increase from $668.8 million in the prior year period, driven by strong performances from the Offshore Projects Group and Subsea Robotics segments.
  • Gross Margin: $148.4 million, up from $120.2 million, indicating improved efficiency and favorable service mix.
  • Operating Income: $79.2 million, compared to $60.4 million in the prior year, primarily due to higher-margin backlog conversion in Manufactured Products and increased ROV day rates.
  • Net Income: $54.4 million, up from $35.0 million, benefiting from recent contract awards in Aerospace and Defense Technologies and strong execution in Offshore Projects Group.
  • Diluted EPS: $0.54, compared to $0.34, reflecting improved operating results and higher average revenue per day in Subsea Robotics.

Business Highlights

  • Subsea Robotics Performance: The Subsea Robotics segment, a major contributor to the Energy business, reported an increase in operating income for the three- and six-month periods ended June 30, 2025, driven by higher average revenue per day. The segment maintained a fleet of 250 work-class ROVs with a utilization rate of 67% for both the three- and six-month periods.
  • Manufactured Products Segment: This segment saw an increase in operating results for the three-month period ended June 30, 2025, due to the execution of higher-margin backlog through energy manufacturing plants. However, the six-month period results were slightly impacted by a $13 million inventory reserve related to the theme park ride business.
  • Offshore Projects Group (OPG) Performance: OPG experienced significant improvements in operating results for the three- and six-month periods ended June 30, 2025, due to higher-margin well intervention projects and improved vessel utilization in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico. The segment operates several deepwater vessels under a mix of short-term and long-term charters.
  • Integrity Management & Digital Solutions (IMDS): The IMDS segment reported increased operating results for the three- and six-month periods ended June 30, 2025, on relatively flat revenue compared to the prior year, indicating improved operational efficiency.
  • Aerospace and Defense Technologies (ADTech) Growth: The ADTech segment reported increased operating results for the three- and six-month periods ended June 30, 2025, due to the commencement of several recent contract awards, leading to increased activity and margins in the defense subsea technologies business.
  • Geographical Performance: The company reported stable foreign revenue, with notable contributions from regions such as Africa, the United Kingdom, Brazil, and Norway. The U.S. market also showed strong performance, contributing significantly to the overall revenue.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued growth in its Energy business, driven by global energy demand and expansion in integrity management, survey services, and mobile robotics. The company is also assessing the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act on its operations and tax provisions.

SEC Filing: OCEANEERING INTERNATIONAL INC [ OII ] - 10-Q - Jul. 24, 2025

data.title: Deckers shares rally as some of the fog clears on the year ahead
data.description:
By Bill Peters

Ugg and Hoka brands notch sales gains, but execs warn 'uncertainty remains elevated in the global trade environment'

Shares of Deckers Outdoor Corp. jumped 15% after hours on Thursday after the footwear maker reported fiscal first-quarter results that beat estimates, helped by big gains in its Hoka sneakers and Ugg shoes.

The company (DECK) reported those results - along with a solid second-quarter guidance - after it said in May that it wouldn't offer a forecast for its entire fiscal year, citing uncertainty caused by "evolving" tariff rates and trade negotiations between the U.S. and the rest of the world.

But on Thursday, Deckers said first-quarter revenue climbed 17% to $965 million, well above FactSet analyst forecasts for $900.4 million. The company earned 93 cents a share, above analyst estimates for 68 cents.

Sales for Hoka were up 19.8% during the quarter, while Ugg sales rose 18.9%. However, sales of the company's other brands - which include Teva and AHNU - fell.

For its second quarter, Deckers said it expects sales of $1.38 billion to $1.42 billion, above FactSet forecasts for $1.403 billion. Management projected earnings per share of $1.50 to $1.55, compared with estimates for $1.51.

"Though uncertainty remains elevated in the global trade environment, our confidence in our brands has not changed, and the long-term opportunities ahead are significant," Chief Executive Stefano Caroti said in a statement.

More nations could face larger tariffs if they can't negotiate trade deals with the U.S. by Aug. 1. However, that deadline is an extension from an earlier one by the Trump administration, and markets have calmed since the U.S. threatened in April to upend the global trade order with higher tariffs on countries across the world.

Deckers, in its most recent annual report, said that less than 5% of its finished footwear came from China, the main target of President Donald Trump's trade war. The rest came from Southeast Asia, mainly Vietnam.

Shares of Deckers were down 48.3% this year as of Thursday's close.

-Bill Peters

data.title: Odds of Berkshire Hathaway Bid for CSX Are Rising; How Buffett Might Try to Pull It Off — Barrons.com
data.description:
Andrew Bary

The odds that Berkshire Hathaway makes a bid for CSX have risen in the past 24 hours.

Union Pacific said earlier Thursday that it's in "advanced discussions" with Norfolk Southern on a potential merger that would create a transcontinental railroad.

CSX's CEO Joseph Hinrichs stated on the company's earnings conference call late Wednesday that the company is "absolutely focused on delivering shareholder value" and that the company is "open to anything that can help us achieve that objective."

It's now up to Berkshire, which owns the Burlington Northern Santa Fe railroad, to decide if it wants to try to purchase CSX, which would create a second transcontinental railroad. Union Pacific and BNSF dominate freight traffic West of the Mississippi River and Norfolk Southern and CSX in the East.

If Berkshire does move, it's unlikely to act in a way that most companies behave in merger situations.

Based on his prior behavior and public statements, Berkshire CEO Warren Buffett refuses to use investment bankers — unlike most companies — or get involved in bidding wars. He prefers to make take-it-or-leave cash offers to corporate boards. That's what happened with Alleghany, the insurer that Berkshire bought in 2022. Buffett thinks investment bankers are expensive and generally useless.

When Alleghany told Berkshire that it wouldn't evaluate Berkshire's offer without consulting its bankers at Goldman Sachs, Buffett cut the price that Berkshire would pay for Alleghany by over $25 million to account for the fee.

Buffett also doesn't like deal terms to leak publicly and he is willing to scotch a deal if that happens.

So if Berkshire does make a move on CSX, look for an all-cash offer that includes a go-shop period that would allow the railroad to consider other offers. That's what happened with Alleghany.

A spokeswoman for Berkshire executive Greg Abel, who heads the company's non-insurance operations, including BNSF, said he declines to comment.

A Berkshire deal for CSX likely would be well received by Berkshire holders who have been waiting for Buffett to execute what he calls an elephant-sized deal that would absorb a chunk of its cash. And buying CSX would have strategic benefits for BNSF, one of Berkshire's most important units.

Berkshire has the cash if it wants to buy CSX, which could cost over $80 billion. Berkshire is sitting on over $300 billion of cash including about $90 billion at its holding company that doesn't carry any potential regulatory restriction on usage that occur with the larger amount of cash at its insurance units. Buffett generally hates to use Berkshire stock as a currency for a deal. He views every share as precious.

Wall Street seems to be taking a wait-and-see approach to potential rail combinations because no deal has been announced and any deal likely would draw lengthy antitrust and regulatory scrutiny.

Norfolk Southern shares were down 0.7% Thursday to $278, but are up about 5% since The Wall Street Journal reported last Thursday that the company was in preliminary talks with Union Pacific. CSX shares gained 0.1% Thursday to $35.01 while Union Pacific stock fell 4.5% to $220.50 Thursday.

Union Pacific stock likely is under pressure in part because investors fear it may have to pay up to get Norfolk Southern and issue a sizable amount of equity in any transaction.

This isn't the kind of deal environment, however, that Buffett likes. CFRA analyst Cathy Seifert said last week that Berkshire doesn't like to "chase a deal." Buffett prefers to act when he wants, but the problem for Berkshire is that if Union Pacific reaches a deal with Norfolk Southern, Berkshire may need to act quickly to get both transactions reviewed simultaneously by regulators.

CSX also wouldn't come cheaply. Its shares now trade for about 20 times projected 2026 earnings. Assume a 25% premium and Berkshire would pay about 25 times earnings — above the multiple of around 15 that it paid when it purchased BNSF in 2010. That would cost about $81 billion.

Even with that premium, a deal would be about 8% accretive to Berkshire's 2026 earnings assuming cost and other synergies, according to UBS analyst Brian Meredith, who follows Berkshire and consulted with the firm's rail analysts for the calculation.

A Berkshire deal for CSX is looking like a distinct possibility, and any deal would show the distinctive way that Buffett operates.

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

data.title: Lucid Launches Alliance With 4 US Mineral Producers
data.description:
Lucid forms an alliance with 4 North American miners to secure critical minerals for U.S. EV production.

The collaboration brings together Lucid and Alaska Energy Metals, Graphite One, Electric Metals and RecycLiCo to launch the Minerals for National Automotive Competitiveness Collaboration.

It officially kicked off with a roundtable in Washington, D.C., attended by Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs, Alaska Senator Dan Sullivan and Reps. Nick Begich and Andy Biggs.

The initiative aims to lock down offtake agreements for lithium, graphite and recycled materials in domestic auto manufacturing, tackle commercialization barriers, boost customer adoption, strengthen coordination between mining and automotive sectors and qualify U.S.?produced inputs for OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers.

Interim CEO Marc Winterhoff said, We are doubling down on our commitment to powering American innovation and look forward to working with our partners as we build the next generation of automotive manufacturing in the United States.

Lucid Group faces a skeptical outlook based on the chart above, with its average 12-month price target set at $2.68, down roughly 10% from current levels.

Though the most bullish scenario sees Lucid stock reaching $7, the bearish case warns of a slide to just $1.

data.title: These Stocks Moved the Most Today: Tesla, Alphabet, IBM, Dow Inc., Honeywell, UnitedHealth, Southwest, American, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel and Mackenzie Tatananni

Stocks were mixed Thursday and the Dow Jones Industrial Average declined after Honeywell and IBM slumped on quarterly earnings updates, and as UnitedHealth Group traded lower after saying it was "complying with formal criminal and civil requests" from the U.S. Department of Justice.

These stocks were moving Thursday:

Tesla fell 8.3% after reporting a 16% decline in net income on a 12% drop in revenue to $22.5 billion. Revenue from Tesla's automotive business and energy business declined 16% and 7%, respectively. Tesla CEO Elon Musk said on the earnings call that production had begun on a less expensive EV and that sales could start in the fourth quarter. He also discussed plans for Tesla to "greatly expand" its robo-taxi service, saying the service should cover about "half the U.S. population" by the end of the year.

Second-quarter earnings at Alphabet topped analysts' estimates and sent shares of the parent of Google up 1.6%. Alphabet reported earnings of $2.31 a share on revenue of $96.43 billion, beating calls for earnings of $2.19 a share on revenue of $93.99 billion. Cloud revenue in the period jumped to $13.62 billion from $10.35 billion a year earlier. Alphabet said it was raising its capital-expenditure plans for the year, targeting $85 billion, up from a previous call for $75 billion. Chief Executive Sundar Pichai attributed the move to the "strong and growing demand for our cloud products and services."

International Business Machines declined 8.1% even after the technology giant reported second-quarter adjusted earnings of $2.80 a share, above the $2.65 a share analysts had anticipated. Revenue of $17 billion beat expectations of $16.6 billion. IBM said it expects at least 5% revenue growth in constant currency in 2025, and now expects to generate more than $13.5 billion in free cash flow. This represents an increase from April, when IBM said it expected to generate about $13.5 billion. Coming into the report, IBM shares had risen 28% this year.

UnitedHealth was down 4% after the health insurance company said it had "full confidence in its practices" and was " committed to working cooperatively" with the DOJ. The Wall Street Journal earlier this year reported the Justice Department was investigating the company's Medicare billing practices, but UnitedHealth hadn't formally confirmed the news.

Union Pacific fell 4.1% and Norfolk Southern was down 0.5% after the two railroad companies said they were "engaged in advanced discussions regarding a potential business combination." Earlier Thursday, Union Pacific posted solid second-quarter earnings that topped analysts' forecasts.

T-Mobile US rose 5.9% after the telecommunications company reported better-than-expected second-quarter earnings and revenue and raised guidance for postpaid net customer additions. For the year, T-Mobile said it now expects postpaid net customer additions of between 6.1 million and 6.4 million, an increase from prior guidance of 5.5 million to 6 million.

Enterprise-software company ServiceNow reported better-than-expected second-quarter adjusted earnings and revenue and raised its full-year subscription revenue guidance as demand for its artificial-intelligence-powered enterprise software remained strong. The stock was rising 3.7%.

Honeywell International fell 5.5%. The industrial conglomerate delivered a solid beat-and-raise in the second quarter as it prepares to break into three pieces. Honeywell reported adjusted earnings of $2.75 a share on sales of $10.4 billion. Analysts were expecting earnings of $2.66 on sales of $10.1 billion.

Dow Inc. plunged 19% after the chemicals company slashed its dividend to 35 cents a share from 70 cents and reported an adjusted loss in the second quarter of 42 cents a share, wider than analysts' estimates that called for a loss of 17 cents.

Southwest Airlines was down 11% after posting second-quarter adjusted earnings of 43 cents a share, below analysts' calls for 51 cents, on revenue of $7.24 billion that also came up short. But the carrier said industry demand "shows signs of improvement off of depressed second quarter 2025 levels, which combined with moderated capacity across the industry and Southwest-specific initiatives, creates a constructive backdrop for the second half of the year."

American Airlines reported adjusted earnings of 95 cents a share in the second quarter, beating estimates of 78 cents. However, the airline said it now expects full-year earnings of between a loss of 20 cents a share and a profit of 80 cents. Wall Street expected 72 cents. American said the "top end of the range is achievable if demand in the domestic market continues to strengthen and only expects to be at the bottom end of the range if there were to be macro weaknesses that are not seen today." Shares fell 9%.

Chipotle Mexican Grill, the burrito chain, sank 14% after posting second-quarter profit and revenue that both met analysts' expectations but same-store sales for existing restaurants that declined 4% from a year earlier. For 2025, Chipotle expects comparable sales flat with 2o24.

Las Vegas Sands earned an adjusted 79 cents a share in the second quarter, beating analysts' estimates of 53 cents. Revenue of $3.18 billion topped consensus of $2.84 billion. Shares of the casino and resort operator were up 4.7%.

American Eagle Outfitters rose 3.7% after the retailer announced that Hollywood actress Sydney Sweeney would headline its Fall 2025 promotional campaign.

West Pharmaceutical Services soared 20%. The maker of injectable medicines reported better-than-anticipated second-quarter earnings and raised its full-year guidance.

Earnings reports are expected after the closing bell Thursday from Intel, Newmont, and VeriSign.

data.title: STMicroelectronics to Buy NXP's MEMS Sensor Business for $950M
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

STMicroelectronics is acquiring NXP Semiconductors' business focused on automotive and industrial sensors for $950 million in cash.

The European semiconductor company said Thursday the acquisition will allow it to further expand its position in the sensor market, particularly in automotive safety sensors.

The company said the acquisition will be financed with existing liquidity, with $900 million paid up front and $50 million subject to the achievement of technical milestones.

The transaction will close in the first half of 2026.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1510 ET - Intel may have more room to cut capital and operating expenses, according to Bank of America's Vivek Arya. Intel said in April that it would reduce its gross capital spending target by $2 billion this year to $18 billion. "However, we see potential for CEO Tan to further revise opex/capex down by 10-15%, opex toward $16bn vs. current $17bn, and capex toward $14-15bn vs. $18bn," Arya writes ahead of the company's earnings report. "While these measures could bring semblance of profit in the near-term," Arya notes concerns including "constant roadmap changes" and employee churn. Arya expects Intel's 2Q results to benefit from tariff pull-ins and a more favorable mix, but the chip company may face a tougher 2H as benefits reverse. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1255 ET - Vodafone Group's top-line performance in Germany exceeded market expectations, with further improvement anticipated, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. The acceleration in German service revenue growth was mainly driven by a sharp and positive turnaround in the wireless segment, they note. Mobile-service revenue in Germany also showed notable improvement, they say. The key driver behind this recovery was the continued migration of 1&1 MVNO customers onto the Vodafone's network from Telefonica Deutschland, they add. Vodafone shares are up 3.4% at 86.02 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1137 ET - Vodafone Group's improving performance in Germany is likely to boost market confidence, Berenberg analysts write in a note. The German segment was the main positive in the company's results, with signs of recovery in service revenue, they say. Additionally, the U.K.-based telecom group reiterated its fiscal 2026 guidance, incorporating the recent merger with rival Three, which was completed last month. Overall, Vodafone delivered a marginally positive update, they add. Shares are up 3.6% at 86.18 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1106 ET - Vodafone Group's unchanged guidance has reassured investors, AJ Bell's Russ Mould writes in a note. The U.K.-based telecommunication group reaffirmed its fiscal 2026 targets for adjusted Ebitda after leases and adjusted free cash flow, supported by growth across all areas of the business except Germany, he notes. The region remains Vodafone's core market, and its continued struggles are a concern, he says. However, the company appears to be making progress in stemming loses there, he adds. Shares are up 4.1% at 86.56 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1046 ET - ITV continues to prove its resilience despite mounting competition, AJ Bell's Russ Mould writes in a note. While critics argue that ITV is becoming less relevant and facing pressure from giants like Netflix, the U.K. broadcaster is holding firm, he says. The company is demonstrating that it still has plenty to offer, he adds. ITV delivered a better-than-expected performance for the first half, supported by the strength of digital advertising, he notes. "The ITVX streaming platform has been a great success and the company is finding new ways to help advertisers reach a more targeted audience," he adds. Shares are up 13.2% at 87.75 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0559 ET - RELX's performance looks solid in a set of first-half results marked by change in the company's reporting model that excludes print revenue from underlying growth, UBS analysts says in a research note. The U.K. information and analytics group reported first-half underlying revenue growth of 7% excluding print, which fell 21% and now represents 4% of the company's revenue, UBS says. "We believe RELX  is on a path to deliver 8% underlying growth--potentially by 2026--as now defined, alongside continuous margin expansion and large buybacks," the analysts say. RELX's adjusted operating profit was in line with consensus expectations, but its margin was ahead, UBS says. Shares trade 0.7% lower. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0437 ET - Vodafone Group's first-quarter results stand out for the lack of significant negatives, Barclays analysts write in a note. The U.K. telecommunication group delivered service revenue ahead of consensus in Germany, Europe, and at the group level, they note. Germany--which has been absorbing the impact from a regulatory change in the country--is slowly recovering, they say. However, the group remains behind its European peers, they add. Shares are up 2% at 84.84 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0357 ET - STMicroelectronics' second-quarter results signal that the recovery in demand for some semiconductors is being hindered by tariff-related uncertainty and weakness in the automotive market, Citi analysts write in a note to clients. The European chip maker said demand for chips in personal electronics and industrial equipment had improved in the second quarter, but demand from automotive clients was below expectations. Chief Executive Jean-Marc Chery said that while overall sales should improve in the third quarter from the second quarter, the company was still operating in an uncertain environment. STMicroelectronics shares trade 12% lower at 23.67 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0353 ET - Nokia's third-quarter guidance for sequentially flat sales and stable operating margin implies a heavily-weighted fourth quarter, JPMorgan analysts Sandeep Deshpande and Craig A McDowell write. The guidance implies a 6% cut to third-quarter revenue and a 42% trim to operating-profit estimates, the bank says. Despite bright spots in the report, including strong order growth with cloud-infrastructure providers, second-quarter results show signs of exposure to weaker markets and operational challenges. JPMorgan sees little in the full report to support the shares, given the heavily weighted fourth quarter performance. "For 2025, the mid-point of the lowered operating profit guidance is 10.6% below Infront consensus expectations - we would expect consensus to move down by at least this level." Shares rise 0.3%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0349 ET - Infosys is likely to see steady earnings growth, based on its FY 2026 revised guidance, Axis Securities analysts say in a research report. The Indian digital services and consulting company slightly adjusted its FY 2026 revenue growth guidance to 1%-3% in constant currency terms from its previous guidance of 0%-3%, the analysts note. The company doesn't foresee any significant changes in 2Q FY 2026 and expects performance to stay at similar levels, which suggests continued caution amid a challenging macroeconomic environment. Moreover, management expects 2H FY 2026 to be weaker than 1H FY 2026 owing to normal seasonality. The brokerage resumes its coverage of the stock with a hold rating and a target price of INR1,680.00. Shares are 1.4% lower at INR1,551.70. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0058 ET - MediaTek's earnings are likely to gain from expanded customer reach in the artificial-intelligence application specific integrated circuit market, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say. The company appears to be on track to ramp up its first AI ASIC project for a U.S.-based cloud service provider. The project is expected to generate US$1 billion revenue in 2026, the analysts say in a note. The Taiwanese chip designer is adding one more U.S.-based CSP to its AI ASIC portfolio, which could triple this business revenue to US$3 billion by 2027. The brokerage upgrades the stock's rating to buy from outperform and raises the target price to NT$1,666.00 from NT$1,550.00. Shares are unchanged at NT$1,430.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0051 ET - The worst is likely over for ASMPT, Citi analysts say in a research note. The semiconductor company has delivered a large thermocompression-bonding tool order to a leading memory chip maker, they say, while another client has begun using ASMPT's bonding tool for low-volume production of 12-layer HBM4 chips. Order volume could become more meaningful in 2026 and 2027 as next-generation AI chips ramp up, they say. Mainstream semiconductor outlook is also improving, driven by AI data centers and EV demand in China, the analysts say. Citi says that new orders for ASMPT's thermocompression-bonding tool could be positive catalysts for its share price. Citi maintains a buy call on ASMPT and raises its target price to HK$75.00 from HK$65.00. Shares are last at HK$69.00. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0251 ET - Roche's keeping its 2025 guidance looks reassuring, Vontobel analyst Sibylle Bischofberger writes in a research note. The Swiss drugmaker booked revenue in line with analysts' expectations for the first half, and said it still sees sales growth in the mid-single digits for the year as a whole. First-half performance is "well within" that guidance, Bischofberger says. "Importantly, despite the current uncertain political environment from the U.S., Roche maintains its 2025 guidance," she says. Vontobel keeps a 303 Swiss francs price target and a buy rating on Roche stock. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

2359 ET - IHH Healthcare's integration of its India units, Fortis and Gleneagles Healthcare India, may enhance operational efficiency and drive long-term growth, says MBSB Research in a note. Fortis will manage five of the six hospitals within the GHI network. By integrating GHI's operations, Fortis can leverage its extensive network to improve procurement, implement better protocols, and provide consistent patient care, it says. The integration is expected to strengthen IHH's presence in India and unlock growth potential through operational synergies. Despite potential risks such as cultural differences and regulatory challenges, IHH appears well-positioned to manage them, it says. MBSB has a buy rating and target price of MYR8.18 on the stock, which is 0.3% higher at MYR6.64. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1830 ET - Healthcare providers across the board are feeling the weight of growing medical costs. Molina Healthcare trimmed its full-year guidance again, just weeks after an earlier cut, in part reflecting a recent acceleration in medical-cost trends. Other providers like Elevance Health and United Healthcare have similarly slashed full-year projections in recent months, while Centene pulled its guidance earlier this month. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1823 ET - The EPA is moving closer to approving the popular pesticide dicamba for farmer use in 2026. The agency opened a 30-day comment period for the proposed approval of three Dicamba-based products, including ones made by crop chemical giants Bayer and Syngenta. Dicamba is widely used among cotton and soybean farmers in the southeast who battle tough, glyphosate resistant weeds. A federal court in Arizona in 2024 vacated the EPA's registration of the chemical. The court said that the EPA's restrictions on the herbicides were not enough to prevent them from drifting to neighboring farmers' fields and damaging crops that lack genetically engineered resistance to the chemical. As a result, farmers weren't able to apply the pesticide this year. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1233 ET - Lamb Weston's board is sending a signal that they're going to be disciplined, Bernstein Research's Alexia Howard said. The french-fry maker in June appointed six new independent directors to its board as part of a cooperation agreement with activist investor Jana Partners. In a sign of its discipline, the board unanimously elected to receive FY26 annual cash retainer in shares of restricted stock. Lamb Weston's annual incentive plan for the fiscal year will include a free cash flow target and its long-term incentive plan will include a return on invested capital related metric. "This suggests they're all going to be mindful of using cash and returns to shareholders," Howard says. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1019 ET - The Los Angeles wildfires in January impacted $51.7 billion worth of homes--or roughly 11,000 residential properties--according to Redfin. The report analyzes data from the Los Angeles City Council. The council shared a list of 11,125 residential parcels in the city of Los Angeles that were inspected by the Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety after the wildfires. Many, but not all, were completely destroyed by the fires. Most homes in this analysis were impacted by the Palisades Fire. This analysis excludes the impact of the Eaton Fire, which impacted the Los Angeles suburb of Altadena, meaning the total value of homes destroyed in the January fires likely far exceeds $51.7 billion. Redfin says the typical impacted home had a value of $3.7 million prior to the fires. Nearly 100 properties had a value of over $20 million prior to the fires. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0823 ET - The collapse of UniCredit's takeover offer for Italian peer Banco BPM calls into question how likely many of these transactions will face obstacles as they progress through the execution phase, Mergermarket's Lucinda Guthrie says. The deal was the fourth largest ongoing listed transaction in Europe, after Monte dei Paschi's bid for Mediobanca, the Covestro-Adnoc deal and BBVA's pursuit of Sabadell. The move highlights the complexity of M&A for European banks, the head of the M&A data and intelligence platform says. "Despite this, dealmakers are expecting an active pipeline of up to 50 active situations in the banking space in 2H in Europe, spurred by a softer interest rate environment, the need to digitalise and a fear of missing out as deals materialise in the space," she says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0349 ET - Infosys is likely to see steady earnings growth, based on its FY 2026 revised guidance, Axis Securities analysts say in a research report. The Indian digital services and consulting company slightly adjusted its FY 2026 revenue growth guidance to 1%-3% in constant currency terms from its previous guidance of 0%-3%, the analysts note. The company doesn't foresee any significant changes in 2Q FY 2026 and expects performance to stay at similar levels, which suggests continued caution amid a challenging macroeconomic environment. Moreover, management expects 2H FY 2026 to be weaker than 1H FY 2026 owing to normal seasonality. The brokerage resumes its coverage of the stock with a hold rating and a target price of INR1,680.00. Shares are 1.4% lower at INR1,551.70. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0317 ET - Lloyds Banking Group's underlying performance looks strong as impairments continue to fuel better-than-projected profit, default rates remain low and borrowers remain resilient, Hargreaves Lansdown's Matt Britzman says. "The [market] reaction might be a little muted, though, given the lack of guidance upgrade off the back of a good set of numbers," he writes in a note. The British bank is exposed to a regulatory probe into car loans given its significant share of the market. The Supreme Court is set to make a judgement on this case this month. The fact that the group hasn't taken further provisions for this could be seen as a sign of confidence, he adds. Shares are up 0.5% at 78 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0255 ET - BNP Paribas's solid print was supported by good cost control and strong revenue from its global markets division, JPMorgan says in a research note after the French bank's second-quarter print. Most metrics came in ahead of consensus expectations, analysts write. "France, Belgium and Personal Finance performed well whilst Italy, Arval and Asset Management were a bit more mixed," they note. JPM still sees the lender's targets for this year and next as ambitious and would expect limited earnings per share upgrades at this stage. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0252 ET - Deutsche Bank's pretax profit beat doesn't reflect the true picture, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note after the German bank's second-quarter results. "Low quality revenue beats in [corporate and other] and underwhelming pre-provision performances in Corporate and Private Bank will likely lead to minimal upgrades (if any)," analysts write. The results fail to excite, especially given the recent share-price gains, they add. Its capital ratio and communication around the impact of capital requirement regulation 3--a EU regulation to strengthen financial stability among credit institutions and investment firms--are positive, they note. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0253 ET - Sabadell's new targets are achievable and not overly ambitious, balancing ambition and realism at a key time in the hostile takeover bid by BBVA, RBC's Pablo de la Torre Cuevas says in a note. The Spanish bank raised its target on 2025 return on tangible equity, a profitability measure, to 14.5% from around 14% previously. It is aiming for 16% in 2027 compared with latest consensus of around 14.5%, the analyst says. The bank's refreshed focus on Spain should benefit from the recovery in lending in the country, with continued resilient earnings even amid falling interest rates in Europe. The biggest gap between current consensus and Sabadell's new targets appears to be in total cost of risk as well as in overall balance sheet growth, he adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0252 ET - Sabadell's ambitious new plan alongside a decent earnings beat should be well received by the market, KBW analysts Hugo Cruz and Ben Maher say in a note. The Spanish lender targets return on tangible equity of 16% in 2027 and total returns equivalent to more than 40% of market cap. However, this is a plan that requires growth, though that makes sense given Sabadell's positioning in Spain, the analysts say. The bank also issued better RoTE guidance for 2025 on the back of better operating expenses and cost of risk. Meanwhile, second-quarter net profit of 486 million euros beat consensus expectations by 11% on lower fees, impairments and taxes, they add. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

2305 ET - Macquarie management's confidence in the Australian financial giant's performance encourages UBS analysts, who say its 1Q update is slightly softer than the market was expecting. The UBS analysts tell clients in a note that the update implies a 10% fall in 1Q profit. However, management says the performance is in line with their expectations and influenced by the timing of asset realizations. This offers some reassurance to the UBS analysts, who are starting to see Macquarie deliver a series of misses relative to expectations. Its reliance on commodities and global markets reduces earnings visibility, they warn. UBS has a neutral rating and A$225.00 target price on the stock, which is down 4.4% at A$215.37. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2131 ET - CIMB Group's 2Q results are expected to show stable net interest margins and strong non-interest income, supported by trading and forex income, Maybank IB analyst Desmond Ch'ng says in a note. Asset quality may remain steady, with credit costs anticipated to be benign, he reckons. However, loan growth could be a weak point, as the lender remains cautious on lending to the corporate and commercial sectors due to competitive yields. Ch'ng assumes CIMB's 2025 loan growth to be 4.5%, compared with management's 5%-7% target. Maybank maintains a hold rating on CIMB and keeps target price at MYR7.60. Shares are 0.3% higher at MYR6.69. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1830 ET - Healthcare providers across the board are feeling the weight of growing medical costs. Molina Healthcare trimmed its full-year guidance again, just weeks after an earlier cut, in part reflecting a recent acceleration in medical-cost trends. Other providers like Elevance Health and United Healthcare have similarly slashed full-year projections in recent months, while Centene pulled its guidance earlier this month. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1504 ET - Oil rises for the first time in five sessions with help from expectations of the U.S. reaching a trade deal with the EU, while continuing to trade in a tight range. "The price action indicates market participants are getting increasingly sanguine about the outlook for oil markets, despite the accelerated phase-out of additional voluntary adjustments by OPEC+," Amarpreet Singh of Barclays says in a note. Oil stocks in the U.S., refining cracks and high-frequency trade flows continue to signal robust demand, "while production in the U.S. seems to be rolling over," he adds. "Based on the weekly estimates from the EIA and corporate commentary, we think risks to our U.S. crude oil production forecasts are skewed lower." WTI settles up 1.2% at $66.03 a barrel, and Brent rises 1% to $69.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1426 ET - Oil futures are gaining but off earlier highs after WSJ reports that the Trump administration plans to allow Chevron to again pump oil in Venezuela, citing people familiar with the plan. The U.S. sources heavy crude oil from Venezuela and other countries such as Canada and Mexico. The U.S. imported 170,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan oil in April before Chevron's license expired, according to EIA data. That was down from almost 300,000 b/d in December and January. WTI is up 0.5% at $65.58 a barrel, and Brent is up 0.3% at $68.72.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0957 ET - Oil futures gain ground with signs of U.S. progress toward trade deals taking some of the tariff-related pessimism out of the market. Yesterday's U.S. inventory data showing a 3.2 million barrel crude stock draw is also supportive of prices, Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. Technically September WTI "remains in a sideways bullish type of structure," he says, with major resistance at $66.11 and support at $64.22. WTI is up 0.9% at $65.85 a barrel and Brent is 0.7% higher at $69.01. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0640 ET - While Centrica posted in-line results and reiterated its guidance, consensus estimates have yet to appropriately incorporate the U.K. energy company's sale of the Cygnus gas field, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. While the consensus for earnings per share of 12.6 pence appears to be consistent with company guidance, JPM continues to expect 11.8 pence earnings per share. Centrica swinging to an overall loss highlights the challenging backdrop of the country's energy sector, which has read-across for peers operating in the U.K., such as E.ON and RWE, they say. Shares trade 1.6% higher at 161.25 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0521 ET - Repsol delivered a surprisingly strong beat to consensus expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. Madrid-listed energy company's second-quarter net income of 702 million euros was well ahead of consensus expectations of 499 million euros. The beat was primarily driven by the upstream division, they write. The operating environment also looks supporting going into the third quarter, with refining margins potentially delivering a boost to earnings, they add. Meanwhile, Repsol should be a beneficiary from any short-term outages during the U.S. hurricane season, they write. Outages in the U.S. would limit supply and deliver a boost to margins. Shares trade up 1.4% at 13.385 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0400 ET - TotalEnergies' second-quarter earnings are in line with expectations, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. Cash flow from operations pre-working capital is 5% above expectations, they write. Overall, the sustainability of its $2 billion quarterly buyback will be in focus when the management takes questions from investors later Thursday. Shares trade down 1.4% at 52.58 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0312 ET - TotalEnergies' rising net debt will begin to weigh on investor sentiment, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. Debt rose significantly during the quarter due to a combination of a large capital expenditure plan, $2 billion of net acquisitions and a further working capital build, they write. The French energy company will need to reassure investors that debt will begin to fall in the second half, they write. Investors will not reward buybacks that are paid out of the balance sheet indefinitely, they add. Shares fall 1.7% to 52.41 euros in opening trade. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2349 ET - Citi analyst Paul McTaggart isn't sure how much of Woodside Energy's stronger-than-expected 2Q cost performance can be repeated. He tells clients in a note that it isn't clear how much of the energy producer's lower cost base is due to structural changes, and how much relates to one-off factors. More positively, he points out that Woodside effectively upgraded its 2025 production guidance by 3% given the impact of the Greater Angostura divestment. Citi stays neutral on the stock while it waits on further news on Woodside's efforts to sell down its stake in its Louisiana LNG gas-export infrastructure. It raises the target price by 19% to A$25.50. Shares are up 1.1% to A$25.48. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2245 ET - Petronas Dagangan could experience a mild positive impact from the government's reduction of RON95 fuel prices to 1.99 ringgit a liter from 2.05 ringgit a liter by end-September, CIMB Securities' Nurzulaikha Azali says in a note. The price cut may support incremental demand amid generally inelastic fuel consumption in Malaysia, and retail volumes will likely remain stable, she says. The earnings impact on the gas-station operator is expected to be neutral, as margins are protected under the automatic pricing mechanism, whereby the government reimburses the difference between market and subsidized prices. However, delays in subsidy reimbursements could strain working capital, the analyst adds. CIMB maintains its hold rating and MYR20.90 target price on the stock, which is unchanged at MYR21.00. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2217 ET - Malaysia's recent fiscal support measures, including cash handouts and fuel price cuts, could boost consumer sentiment and support near-term spending, say CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia. The auto, consumer, and REIT sectors, along with Petronas Dagangan, could benefit from increased sales in 2H, they say in a note. However, the analysts don't expect a significant impact on overall KLCI earnings, as the key beneficiaries aren't index heavyweights. CIMB maintains its end-2025 KLCI target at 1560, with 99 Speedmart, Mynews Holdings, Fraser & Neave, and Mr. D.I.Y Group (M) among the top picks in the consumer sector. The KLCI is up 0.1% at 1530.70. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2144 ET - Ampol's performance in its non-refining businesses looks impressive to Macquarie analysts given the challenges it faces from weak fuel volumes and tobacco sales. They write in a note that the Australian company's ability to grow convenience retail Ebit shows discipline in fuel pricing and highlights its premium fuel strategies. They raise their EPS forecast for fiscal 2025 on lower interest and tax expenses, but cut by 3.6% for fiscal 2026 and by 3.2% for fiscal 2027 on expected lower fuel sales volumes. Macquarie keeps a neutral rating and A$27.50 target price on the stock, which is up 0.3% at A$27.85. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2006 ET - Oil rises in the early Asian session amid optimism toward U.S.-EU trade talks. The U.S. and the EU are working toward a deal that would set a 15% tariff for most EU goods to the U.S., say ANZ Research analysts, noting media reports. This follows a similar U.S. deal with Japan, they say in a research report. The prospect of a U.S.-EU trade deal has sparked a risk-on tone and boosted sentiment, the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are up 0.4% at $65.50/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $68.74/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1407 ET - Hopes that negotiations between the EU and US yield a trade deal are spurring optimism, resulting in decreased demand for gold. But investors are keeping a guarded view on the outcome. "Markets are remaining cautious over the trade picture with South Korea and India, which are both still negotiating deals and could face steep tariffs as well," says ADM Investor Services in a note. The firm also notes that strong demand from central banks around the world will also support elevated gold prices. The front-month contract finishes down 0.7% to $3,371 per troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0357 ET - Anglo American's key division reported a solid second-quarter production results, RBC Capital Markets analysts write. It is a challenging reporting period for the miner given the extensive restructuring that is taking place. However, the good news is that production and unit cost guidance for the businesses that it will keep remain unchanged, the analysts write. Copper production was in line with the analysts' expectations while diamond production and refined PGM production was below. Steelmaking coal production of 2.1 million metric tons was 27% ahead of expectations, they write. Shares trade up 0.2% at 2,352 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2351 ET - Petronas Chemicals' 2Q earnings could be hurt by operational issues, weak product prices and global oversupply, CIMB Securities analyst Nurzulaikha Azali says in a note. Earnings are projected to decline 5% sequentially in 2Q, weighed by ongoing operational issues at its plants, as well as continued downtime at the Pengerang Petrochemicals Complex, she says. Challenges persist with declining prices in key segments like olefins and fertilisers, she says. The ramp-up of new capacities, especially in Southeast Asia, is expected to keep pressuring prices lower, she reckons. The overall outlook remains cautious with limited near-term earnings upside, due to structural issues and continued market weakness, she adds. CIMB maintains a hold rating on Petronas Chemicals, with an unchanged target price of MYR3.53. Shares are 0.3% lower at MYR3.36.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2228 ET - Iron ore prices are lower in early Asian trade amid signs of stronger supply. Vale, one of the world's biggest producers, said it produced 83.6 million tons of iron ore in 2Q, the highest 2Q production since 2021. Still, sentiment is broadly positive this week, following the announcement that China will be proceeding with the 1.2 trillion yuan hydropower project, ANZ Research analysts say in a research note. That could provide a massive boost to the concrete and steel sectors, which have been struggling amid a slowdown in the property sector, they add. The most actively traded September iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.7% lower at CNY809.5 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2222 ET - Lynas Rare Earths' 4Q production, sales and prices are better than expected, according to Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan. That is partially offset by disappointments on unit costs and capital expenditure, he says. "Lynas will need to sign strategic, new offtake mechanisms or partnerships to justify current valuations," Ryan says. It "is strategically well positioned to do so, capturing any market strength through production expansions and diversification." Jefferies has a buy rating and A$10.00 target on Lynas. The stock is up 3.0% at A$10.44. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1935 ET - Investors are likely to react positively to Fortescue's strong hematite operational performance and a reduction in energy spending, Citi analyst Paul McTaggart says in a note. Fortescue's FY 2025 iron-ore shipments are a record and 4% higher year over year, while hematite costs edged lower, he notes. Energy spending in FY 2026 is reduced to roughly US$700 million versus about US$1.1 billion in FY 2025. Realized 4Q pricing for Fortescue's hematite product is broadly as expected, McTaggart adds. Citi has a neutral rating and A$16.00 target on Fortescue. The stock ended Wednesday at A$18.21. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1433 ET - O'Reilly Automotive customers are willing to pay higher prices for essential car repairs because it's cheaper than buying a new vehicle, executives say. "The strong value proposition of maintaining and repairing an existing vehicle, coupled with the high quality of vehicles, creates a very compelling incentive for our customers to prioritize their auto part spend," CEO Brad Beckham says during an analyst call. O'Reilly raised prices to mitigate new tariff costs, and increasingly complex car repairs are sending up prices, Beckham says. While there's some pullback on discretionary purchases, consumers are willing to shell out for a new alternator or battery.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1430 ET - O'Reilly Automotive is clocking some uncertainty among consumers despite a broader market recovery from a volatile start to this year. The car part provider says its discretionary category, which makes up a relatively small portion of its business, showed softness in 2Q. "The continued sluggishness in these categories is an indicator that consumers are still remaining cautious and conservative in how they are managing the spend in the current environment," CEO Brad Beckham says on an analyst call. He says O'Reilly's guidance is conservative to account for the possibility that consumers will pull back more in the second half of the year if prices rise. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1419 ET - O'Reilly Automotive is worried about inflation. Management is keeping a close eye on how tariffs raise prices across other industries because it is concerned that will put pressure on how much consumers are willing to spend on non-essential repairs like fixing their air conditioning or getting an oil change, CFO Jeremy Fletcher says. O'Reilly is logging pressure in its discretionary category, despite strength elsewhere in the business. Executives say they are still unsure if inflationary pressure has gotten worse in recent months. Low-income consumers are particularly squeezed. "Our goal is to minimize any impact to our consumer because we know they're under pressure," President Brent Kirby says. O'Reilly is up 4% after raising its sales outlook for the year as it logged higher 2Q revenue.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1356 ET - The cost to ship grains from a port in the U.S. Gulf to Japan reached the highest point since last year, reflecting robust export sales globally. According to the USDA's Grain Transportation report, the rate for shipping a metric ton of grain from the Gulf to Japan was $52.75 as of July 17. That's the highest rate since last October and up $4 from the previous week--the biggest uptick in nearly 2 years, says the USDA. President Trump announced a trade deal with Japan this week, which includes many agriculture-centric provisions. CBOT grains are mostly lower in today's session, with soybeans down 0.1% and wheat off 0.3% while corn rises 0.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1306 ET - American Airlines and Southwest Airlines, carriers with the heaviest exposure to domestic travel, are taking the brunt of a selloff in U.S. airline stocks. Southwest, the nation's largest carrier of domestic passengers, reported lower 2Q revenue and earnings after sluggish domestic flying earlier in the year stabilized but failed to rebound. American reports slightly higher 2Q revenue but says weak domestic leisure travel offset higher premium and international demand. American gets about 70% of its revenue from domestic flying, which is outsized exposure compared with its network peers, Melius Research analysts Conor Cunningham and Patrick Coleman say in a research note. Southwest slides 13% to while American drops 10%. Delta Air Lines is off 2.3% while United Airlines is flat. Smaller carriers are seeing 3% to 6% stock declines. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1243 ET - American Airlines is taking a conservative approach to its guidance for the remainder of the year despite indications of improving U.S. travel demand, Melius Research analysts Conor Cunningham and Patrick Coleman say in a research note. Investors don't seem happy with the outlook, with shares now down 8.3% at $11.63. Delta Air Lines and United Airlines have both called out a sequential uptick in July bookings from June as the economic backdrop becomes less uncertain, the analysts say. American is reporting a similar rate of change, but taking a more cautious view of how it will unfold in the back half of 2025, they say. The carrier was disproportionately hurt by the slowdown in demand earlier this year given its outsized exposure to domestic travel, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1202 ET - Tesla's long-term upside is largely predicated on its plans for lower cost cars, autonomous vehicles and AI products. But it offered few updates to these critical releases with its 2Q results, Truist analysts say. "At a minimum, considering they are already supposedly in production, we expected the company to describe aspects of its new lower-cost models (cost, features, estimated production, etc.) Alas, management offered no such details," the analysts say, adding that the company also said little about its autonomous vehicles, Optimus robot or Dojo supercomputer. Tesla also didn't provide any meaningful future financial guidance, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1149 ET - To fund its long-term bets in AI and autonomous vehicles, Tesla is going to need cash. But the company only generated $146 million in free cash flow in 2Q, down from $1.34 billion the previous year. That could be a warning sign for its future cash reserves, say Truist analysts in a research note. "Considering the turbulence from tariffs, the reduction in consumer & regulatory credits, and ongoing capex needs, management acknowledged that the next few quarters could get worse, and potentially drain some of the company's $37b cash position," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1146 ET - Tesla's EV sales are falling, but that shouldn't matter much if it hits its goals for autonomous vehicles, which it wants to make available to half the U.S. population by year-end. Wedbush analysts estimate autonomous vehicles could be a $1 trillion opportunity for Tesla and that its laser-focus on AI will reward investors who stick with the company through the EV struggles. "Musk now entering the picture as a wartime CEO to put TSLA on an aggressive AI-focused strategy represents the biggest and best possible news for Tesla investors," the analysts say. Wedbush maintains its outperform rating and $500 price target. Shares drop 9.1% to $302.29. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0843 ET - American Airlines shares are under pressure after the company offered a dim outlook for the current quarter. The company plans for capacity to be up 2% to 3%, which isn't far from the consensus view for a 3% gain, TD Cowen analysts say in a research note. But it also says revenue could rise as much as 1% or drop as much as 2%. The consensus view was that revenue would rise up to half a percentage point, the analysts say. The revenue outlook implies that revenue per available seat mile, a metric for capacity utilization, will be down 3%, they say. American also expects to post a 3Q adjusted loss of 10 cents to 60 cents a share. The analyst consensus was for a tiny 1-cent adjusted loss, according to TD Cowen. The analysts point out that American has consistently outperformed its guidance after changing up its leadership last year. Shares slide 6%. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0604 ET - China's upcoming Politburo meeting could release a consolidated plan to reduce excess competition in key industries, Minsheng Securities analysts say in a note. The campaign will likely target EV and solar industries and focus on regulating behaviors of both local governments and companies, they say. The meeting is expected to show the progress of the government's economic plan from 2026 to 2030. While stimulus plans are unlikely to be imminent, as 1H growth was steady, the meeting may provide clues on potential incremental policy measures, especially for property and consumption sectors, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0532 ET - JPMorgan expects Kuehne + Nagel to see a downward revision to consensus estimates given that the 2025 EBIT guidance has been lowered around 5% at midpoint, analysts say in a research note. The Swiss logistics company posted second-quarter earnings before interest and taxes of 342 million Swiss francs, coming in 6% below the company-compiled consensus. As the guidance downgrade was broadly expected, volume growth provides some supportive second-quarter comparatives for peers, the analysts say. Overall, the analysts say they continue to view the backdrop as challenging, with consensus estimates under risk. Shares trade 0.5% higher at 173.95 Swiss francs. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

data.title: Edwards Lifesciences raises annual sales forecast on strong demand for heart devices
data.description:
Edwards Lifesciences raised its 2025 sales forecast on Thursday, citing strong demand for its artificial heart valves and other medical devices, after posting better-than-expected results for the second quarter.

Shares of the California-based company rose about 8% in extended trading.

Investor confidence in medical device makers has climbed in recent quarters, underpinned by robust demand for surgical procedures, particularly among older adults.

Edwards now expects full-year sales in the range of $5.9 billion to $6.1 billion, having raised the lower end of its previous forecast from $5.7 billion while maintaining the upper end.

Earlier on Thursday, larger rival Boston Scientific also raised its annual profit forecast, buoyed by strong demand for its heart devices.

Sales of Edwards' flagship transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) devices, used in minimally invasive heart surgeries, jumped 8.9% to $1.13 billion in the quarter ended June 30, topping analysts' average estimate of $1.09 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

On an adjusted basis, Edwards earned 67 cents per share in the second quarter, beating Wall Street's estimate of 62 cents per share.

It reported revenue of $1.53 billion, surpassing market expectation of $1.49 billion.

The company said it now expects its 2025 adjusted earnings to come in at the high end of its earlier forecast range of $2.40 to $2.50 per share.

Edwards said its forecast factors in current tariffs and warned that any changes or new tariffs could significantly impact future results.

data.title: Trump administration sues New York City to block immigration 'sanctuary' laws
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump administration sues to block laws limiting immigration enforcement
  • Lawsuit follows Trump officials criticizing NYC Mayor Adams
  • Democrats push back on Trump's aggressive raids

The Trump administration on Thursday sued New York City over local laws that limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, saying the statutes unlawfully thwart immigration arrests and conflict with federal immigration law.

In a complaint filed in Brooklyn federal court, the U.S. government said New York City's "sanctuary provisions" are unconstitutional and preempted by laws giving it authority to regulate immigration.

President Donald Trump, a Republican seeking to deport millions of immigrants in the U.S. illegally, has sparred with New York and other Democratic strongholds over laws limiting cooperation with federal immigration enforcement. Democrats, in turn, have criticized the Trump administration's aggressive enforcement tactics, including plainclothes immigration agents covering their faces to hide their identities and arrests of immigrants with no criminal records.

The lawsuit cites a recent incident where two migrants in the U.S. illegally allegedly attempted to rob an off-duty customs officer in a city park, shooting him in the face and leg in the process.

"New York City has long been at the vanguard of interfering with enforcing this country’s immigration laws," the complaint reads. "Its history as a sanctuary city dates back to 1989, and its efforts to thwart federal immigration enforcement have only intensified since."

The defendants include Mayor Eric Adams, who is seeking reelection in November. The New York City mayor's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

In a stunning move, the U.S. Justice Department moved in February to drop corruption charges against Adams, saying the case was making it harder for the mayor to crack down on illegal immigration. The federal judge overseeing the case said in April that the deal "smacks of a bargain."

Adams, who began his first term in 2022 as a Democrat, plans to run as an independent. He will go up against frontrunner Zohran Mamdani, a liberal Democrat who won the party's nomination in June.

Trump officials this week ripped Adams during a press conference in the city following the incident injuring the customs officer.

"Start looking at the candidates today and see which one is going to start making the city safer, because you've got a mayor today that could have done better," Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said.

In the lawsuit, the U.S. Justice Department said that in fiscal year 2024, New York City jails only honored 4% of federal requests to hold inmates so that they could be picked up for immigration violations.

Prominent Democrats in New York have pushed back on Trump's immigration crackdown. Federal immigration officers arrested city comptroller Brad Lander in June while he was escorting a defendant out of immigration court and detained him for several hours.
